mechanism_of_action: Decreases glucose production in the liver and improves insulin sensitivity in body tissues.
dosage_common: 500mg once or twice daily with meals
overdose_effects: Severe lactic acidosis, weakness, slow heart rate, muscle pain.
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Considered safe, but insulin is often preferred.
kidney_liver_warning: Strictly contraindicated in severe kidney impairment.
how_it_works_in_body: Helps your body make better use of the insulin it naturally produces.
quick_summary: The first-line medication for managing Type 2 Diabetes.
who_should_take: Patients diagnosed with Type 2 Diabetes or PCOS.
who_should_not_take: Patients with severe kidney disease.
food_interactions: Must be taken with or immediately after food to prevent severe stomach upset.
alcohol_warning: Avoid excessive alcohol due to increased risk of lactic acidosis.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks the enzyme in the liver responsible for making cholesterol, thereby lowering bad cholesterol (LDL) and raising good cholesterol (HDL).
overdose_effects: Muscle pain, weakness, digestive issues.
pregnancy_safety: Category X - STRICTLY AVOID during pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Contraindicated in active liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Forces the liver to clear LDL (bad cholesterol) from the blood by stopping its internal production.
quick_summary: Highly effective widely-used med to lower cholesterol and prevent heart disease.
who_should_take: Patients with high cholesterol or high cardiovascular risk.
who_should_not_take: Pregnant women and individuals with liver disease.
food_interactions: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice as it increases drug levels in blood.
alcohol_warning: Limit alcohol; heavy consumption increases the risk of liver damage.

mechanism_of_action: Replaces the missing thyroxine hormone that the thyroid gland would naturally produce.
dosage_common: 50mcg once daily on an empty stomach in the morning
overdose_effects: Fast heartbeat, fever, sweating, confusion, nervousness.
pregnancy_safety: Category A - Safe and necessary to continue during pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Safe, but requires regular blood monitoring.
how_it_works_in_body: Restores normal metabolic rates and energy levels in the body.
quick_summary: Essential replacement therapy for underactive thyroid issues.
who_should_take: Patients diagnosed with hypothyroidism.
who_should_not_take: Patients with untreated adrenal gland problems or recent heart attack.
food_interactions: Must be taken on a strictly empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before breakfast. Avoid eating walnuts, soybean flour, or high dietary fiber immediately after.
alcohol_warning: Mild alcohol use is fine, but it can affect medication absorption.

mechanism_of_action: Montelukast blocks leukotrienes (chemicals that cause airway inflammation). Levocetirizine blocks histamine (chemical causing allergy symptoms).
dosage_common: 1 tablet once daily, preferably at night
overdose_effects: Severe drowsiness, abdominal pain, restlessness.
pregnancy_safety: Category B/C - Consult doctor before use.
kidney_liver_warning: Not recommended in severe kidney failure.
how_it_works_in_body: Opens up the airways and prevents the immune system's hyper-reaction to allergens.
quick_summary: A highly effective combination for treating chronic allergies, asthma, and severe runny nose.
who_should_take: Patients with persistent allergies or concurrent asthma.
who_should_not_take: Patients with severe kidney disease.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
alcohol_warning: Avoid completely; heavily increases drowsiness and dizziness.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes, stopping the production of prostaglandins which cause pain and inflammation.
dosage_common: 50mg twice or thrice daily after meals
overdose_effects: Lethargy, stomach pain, vomiting blood, shallow breathing.
pregnancy_safety: Category C (D in 3rd trimester) - Avoid, especially later in pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Avoid in severe kidney disease. Use with caution in liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Provides strong relief from swelling, stiffness, and severe pain.
quick_summary: A strong painkiller and anti-inflammatory used for joint and severe muscle issues.
who_should_take: People suffering from arthritis, sports injuries, or intense muscle aches.
who_should_not_take: Patients with stomach ulcers, asthma, or kidney disease.
food_interactions: Always take with food or milk to prevent stomach ulcers and bleeding.
alcohol_warning: Strictly avoid; hugely increases the risk of fatal stomach bleeding.

mechanism_of_action: Pantoprazole reduces stomach acid. Domperidone increases movement of the stomach and intestines, allowing food to move more easily.
dosage_common: 1 capsule once daily, 30 minutes before breakfast
overdose_effects: Confusion, tremors, irregular heartbeat.
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Consult doctor before use.
kidney_liver_warning: Dose adjustment needed in severe liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Decreases the amount of acid produced in the stomach and prevents nausea/vomiting.
quick_summary: Commonly used for severe acidity, acid reflux, and heartburn.
who_should_take: Patients with GERD or chronic acidity.
who_should_not_take: Patients with severe liver impairment.
food_interactions: Take on an empty stomach.
alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it increases stomach acid and worsens symptoms.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis in the central nervous system to reduce pain and fever.
overdose_effects: Liver failure, yellowing of eyes/skin, severe nausea, dark urine.
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Generally safe during pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with extreme caution in liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Acts on the brain's temperature control center to lower fever and elevates the pain threshold.
quick_summary: A widely used, safe medication for reducing fever and relieving mild pain.
who_should_take: People with fever, headache, or mild body aches.
who_should_not_take: People with severe liver disease.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
alcohol_warning: Strictly avoid heavy alcohol use; significantly increases risk of liver damage.

mechanism_of_action: Cetirizine blocks histamine (allergy symptoms). Paracetamol reduces fever/pain. Phenylephrine narrows blood vessels to relieve nasal congestion.
overdose_effects: Severe drowsiness, rapid heartbeat, liver damage.
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Avoid unless prescribed by a doctor.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in liver and kidney disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Provides multi-symptom relief from cold, fever, and allergies.
quick_summary: Combination medicine used to treat common cold symptoms like blocked nose, runny nose, and fever.
who_should_take: Patients suffering from cold, flu, or severe nasal allergy symptoms.
who_should_not_take: Patients with high blood pressure or severe liver issues.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol; increases drowsiness and risk of liver damage.

mechanism_of_action: Stops bacterial growth by inhibiting their protein synthesis.
overdose_effects: Severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, temporary hearing loss.
contraindications: History of liver problems with this drug
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Generally considered safe.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in severe liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Prevents bacteria from multiplying, allowing the immune system to clear the infection.
quick_summary: A widely used antibiotic for throat, ear, and respiratory infections.
who_should_take: Patients with bacterial infections prescribed by a doctor.
who_should_not_take: Patients with a history of severe liver problems or irregular heartbeats.
food_interactions: Take 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals (for some brands/forms), though many tablets can be taken with food.
alcohol_warning: Alcohol does not directly interact but can worsen side effects like dizziness and stomach upset.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks the action of angiotensin II, a chemical that tightens blood vessels, thereby relaxing them and lowering blood pressure.
overdose_effects: Dizziness, fainting, fast or slow heartbeat.
pregnancy_safety: Category D - STRICTLY AVOID during pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in severe kidney or liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Relaxes blood vessels, allowing blood to flow more smoothly and the heart to pump more efficiently.
quick_summary: A widely prescribed medicine to lower high blood pressure and protect the heart.
who_should_take: Patients with high blood pressure or at high risk of cardiovascular events.
who_should_not_take: Pregnant women and patients with severe liver disease.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol; it can excessively lower blood pressure and increase dizziness.

mechanism_of_action: Drotaverine relieves smooth muscle spasms. Mefenamic acid blocks chemical messengers that cause pain and inflammation.
dosage_common: 1 tablet twice or thrice a day as prescribed
overdose_effects: Severe stomach pain, vomiting blood, extreme dizziness.
pregnancy_safety: Category C/D - Avoid during late pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution; dose adjustment may be needed.
how_it_works_in_body: Relaxes the smooth muscles of the stomach and intestines while reducing pain-causing prostaglandins.
quick_summary: A combination medicine used to treat severe stomach pain and menstrual cramps.
who_should_take: Adults suffering from acute abdominal spasms or severe period pain.
who_should_not_take: Patients with active stomach ulcers or severe kidney/liver issues.
food_interactions: Take with food to avoid stomach upset.
alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it increases the risk of stomach bleeding.

mechanism_of_action: Relaxes the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract by blocking acetylcholine.
side_effects_serious: Decreased sweating leading to heat stroke
overdose_effects: Dilated pupils, hot/dry skin, severe confusion, difficulty swallowing.
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Generally considered safe, but consult a doctor.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in patients with liver or kidney impairment.
how_it_works_in_body: Blocks the action of a chemical messenger (acetylcholine) to relax stomach and gut muscles.
quick_summary: Used to relieve stomach and intestinal cramps, especially in IBS.
who_should_take: Patients experiencing painful gut spasms or IBS symptoms.
who_should_not_take: Patients with glaucoma, severe ulcerative colitis, or difficulty urinating.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it can increase dizziness and drowsiness.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks the release of certain chemical messengers that cause fever, pain, and inflammation.
overdose_effects: Liver failure, severe stomach pain, dark urine.
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Avoid in 3rd trimester.
kidney_liver_warning: Strict caution required; avoid in severe liver/kidney disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Reduces pain and fever by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis in the brain and body.
quick_summary: A common painkiller and fever reducer used for various types of mild to moderate pain.
who_should_take: People with fever, body ache, or menstrual pain.
who_should_not_take: People with a history of stomach ulcers or severe liver issues.
food_interactions: Take with food or milk to prevent stomach upset.
alcohol_warning: Strictly avoid alcohol; increases risk of severe liver damage and stomach bleeding.

mechanism_of_action: Levocetirizine blocks histamine to reduce allergy symptoms. Montelukast blocks leukotrienes to reduce airway inflammation.

overdose_effects: Severe drowsiness, restlessness, stomach pain.
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Consult doctor before use.
kidney_liver_warning: Dose adjustment needed in severe kidney disease.

alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it can increase drowsiness and dizziness.

mechanism_of_action: Ofloxacin kills bacteria by preventing them from reproducing. Ornidazole kills parasites and anaerobic bacteria by damaging their DNA.
dosage_common: 1 tablet twice a day for 3-5 days
overdose_effects: Confusion, dizziness, severe nausea, seizures.
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Avoid during pregnancy, especially first trimester.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in severe liver or kidney disease.

alcohol_warning: STRICTLY AVOID alcohol during treatment and for 3 days after; can cause severe nausea, vomiting, and headache (Disulfiram-like reaction).

mechanism_of_action: Amoxicillin kills bacteria. Clavulanic acid prevents bacteria from destroying Amoxicillin, making it effective against resistant strains.
overdose_effects: Severe stomach pain, vomiting, diarrhea, hyperactivity.
contraindications: History of liver problems with this drug

kidney_liver_warning: Dose adjustment needed in severe kidney disease. Monitor liver function.

who_should_take: Patients with bacterial infections prescribed by a doctor.
who_should_not_take: Anyone with a known allergy to penicillin.

alcohol_warning: Alcohol does not directly interact, but it can worsen side effects like dizziness and stomach upset.

mechanism_of_action: Reduces pain and fever (Paracetamol/Aceclofenac) and breaks down abnormal proteins at the site of inflammation to promote healing (Serratiopeptidase).
dosage_common: 1 tablet twice a day after meals
overdose_effects: Liver failure, severe stomach pain, dizziness.
pregnancy_safety: Category C/D - Avoid during pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with extreme caution in liver or kidney impairment.
how_it_works_in_body: Provides triple action: relieves pain, reduces fever, and actively reduces swelling at the injury site.

who_should_take: Patients with acute pain accompanied by significant swelling.
who_should_not_take: Patients with stomach ulcers or bleeding disorders.
food_interactions: Must be taken with or after food to prevent stomach bleeding and irritation.
alcohol_warning: Strictly avoid alcohol; significantly increases the risk of severe liver damage and stomach bleeding.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks specific abnormal proteins that signal cancer cells to multiply
dosage_common: 400mg to 600mg once daily with a meal
overdose_effects: Severe muscle pain, severe nausea, and vomiting
pregnancy_safety: Category D - Avoid during pregnancy unless absolutely necessary
kidney_liver_warning: Dose adjustment required in liver or kidney impairment

who_should_take: Patients with BCR-ABL positive leukemia or specific stomach tumors.
who_should_not_take: Pregnant women or those with severe liver disease without monitoring.
food_interactions: Avoid grapefruit juice
alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it may increase liver toxicity risk.

mechanism_of_action: Reduces the amount of acid produced in the stomach

overdose_effects: No specific symptoms; usually well-tolerated even in high doses
pregnancy_safety: Generally considered safe; consult doctor
kidney_liver_warning: Safe in kidney disease; use with caution in severe liver disease

who_should_not_take: People with known allergy to PPIs.

alcohol_warning: Alcohol can increase stomach acid production, worsening symptoms.

mechanism_of_action: Amoxicillin kills bacteria; Clavulanic acid prevents bacteria from destroying Amoxicillin
overdose_effects: Stomach pain, vomiting, and diarrhea
contraindications: History of jaundice with this drug
pregnancy_safety: Generally considered safe; consult doctor
kidney_liver_warning: Dose adjustment needed in severe kidney disease

who_should_take: People with bacterial infections as prescribed.
who_should_not_take: People with a known allergy to Penicillin.

alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it may increase side effects like dizziness.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks the action of a substance that narrows blood vessels
uses: Prevention of heart attack and stroke
overdose_effects: Low blood pressure, rapid or slow heartbeat, and dizziness
pregnancy_safety: Category D - Strictly avoid during pregnancy; can harm the fetus
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in liver and kidney disease
how_it_works_in_body: It blocks the binding of angiotensin II to its receptors, which prevents blood vessels from constricting and lowers blood pressure.
quick_summary: Long-acting medicine to control high blood pressure and protect the heart.
who_should_take: Adults with hypertension or high cardiovascular risk.
who_should_not_take: Pregnant women or those with severe bile duct blockage.

alcohol_warning: Alcohol can further lower blood pressure, causing fainting.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks the enzyme in the liver that produces cholesterol
uses: Prevention of heart attack and stroke
overdose_effects: No specific symptoms; risk of liver and muscle damage increases
pregnancy_safety: Category X - Strictly prohibited; causes birth defects
kidney_liver_warning: Avoid in active liver disease; regular liver tests recommended
how_it_works_in_body: It reduces the production of LDL (bad) cholesterol in the liver and increases the liver's ability to remove LDL from the blood.

quick_summary: Commonly used medicine to lower bad cholesterol and prevent heart disease.

who_should_not_take: Pregnant women or those with active liver problems.
food_interactions: Avoid large amounts of grapefruit juice
alcohol_warning: Limit alcohol consumption to avoid liver toxicity.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks histamine receptors to prevent allergic symptoms
dosage_common: 5mg once daily, usually in the evening
overdose_effects: Extreme sleepiness and agitation
pregnancy_safety: Generally considered safe; consult doctor
kidney_liver_warning: Dose adjustment required in kidney disease
how_it_works_in_body: It is the active form of cetirizine and works by blocking H1 histamine receptors more effectively with less sedation.

who_should_take: Adults and children with allergic symptoms.
who_should_not_take: People with end-stage kidney disease.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food
alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it increases drowsiness.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks leukotrienes, which cause swelling in the lungs and tightening of the muscles around the airways
overdose_effects: Abdominal pain, sleepiness, thirst, headache, and vomiting
pregnancy_safety: Generally considered safe; consult doctor
kidney_liver_warning: Safe in kidney disease; use with caution in severe liver disease

quick_summary: Medicine to prevent asthma attacks and treat year-round allergies.

who_should_not_take: People with a history of severe mental health issues related to this drug.

alcohol_warning: No specific interaction, but monitor for mood changes.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis to reduce pain and inflammation
overdose_effects: Vomiting, gastrointestinal bleeding, diarrhea, and dizziness

kidney_liver_warning: Can cause kidney damage with long-term use
how_it_works_in_body: It inhibits the COX-2 enzyme, which is responsible for producing inflammatory chemicals in the body.
quick_summary: Powerful painkiller for joint and muscle inflammation.

quick_summary: Common deworming medicine used to treat various parasitic infections.

alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it may increase liver stress.

uses: Nausea and vomiting caused by chemotherapy
overdose_effects: Sudden blindness (short-lived), severe constipation, and low blood pressure

who_should_not_take: People with congenital long QT syndrome.

alcohol_warning: No major interaction, but alcohol can worsen nausea.

mechanism_of_action: Paralyzes and kills the parasites by interfering with their nervous system
dosage_common: 12mg as a single dose (depending on weight)

quick_summary: Effective medicine for treating various parasitic infections like scabies and worms.

quick_summary: Effective pain reliever, especially for menstrual cramps and fever.
who_should_take: People with dysmenorrhea, fever, or mild to moderate pain.
who_should_not_take: Patients with a history of GI bleeding or severe kidney disease.
food_interactions: Take with food or milk to reduce stomach upset
alcohol_warning: Increases the risk of stomach bleeding.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks the action of leukotrienes, reducing inflammation and bronchoconstriction
overdose_effects: Abdominal pain, somnolence, thirst, headache, vomiting
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Generally considered safe
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in severe liver impairment
how_it_works_in_body: Prevents leukotrienes from causing swelling and tightening of airways.
quick_summary: Prevents asthma attacks and relieves severe allergy symptoms.
who_should_take: Patients with asthma or seasonal allergic rhinitis.
who_should_not_take: People with a history of severe neuropsychiatric symptoms.
alcohol_warning: Safe, but alcohol may worsen underlying asthma/allergy symptoms.

mechanism_of_action: Irreversibly binds to the gastric proton pump, blocking gastric acid secretion
overdose_effects: No specific symptoms, general supportive care needed
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to rabeprazole or other PPIs
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Generally considered safe
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in severe liver impairment
how_it_works_in_body: Reduces the amount of acid produced in the stomach, allowing ulcers to heal.
quick_summary: Strong acid reducer for GERD, ulcers, and severe acidity.
who_should_take: Patients with acid reflux, GERD, or stomach ulcers.
who_should_not_take: People with known hypersensitivity to PPIs.
food_interactions: Take 30 minutes before a meal for maximum effectiveness
alcohol_warning: Alcohol can irritate the stomach lining, worsening acidity.

mechanism_of_action: Acts on the cough center in the medulla to elevate the threshold for coughing
overdose_effects: Nausea, vomiting, stupor, coma, respiratory depression
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Use if potential benefit justifies the risk
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in liver impairment
how_it_works_in_body: Suppresses the urge to cough by acting on the brain.
quick_summary: Effective cough suppressant for dry, non-productive coughs.
who_should_take: Individuals suffering from a dry, hacking cough.
who_should_not_take: Patients with a productive (wet) cough or taking MAOIs.
alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it significantly increases CNS depression.

mechanism_of_action: Non-selective inhibitor of cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2)
overdose_effects: Lethargy, drowsiness, nausea, vomiting, epigastric pain
contraindications: History of asthma triggered by NSAIDs
pregnancy_safety: Category C (Category D in 3rd trimester)
kidney_liver_warning: Avoid in severe kidney impairment
how_it_works_in_body: Reduces inflammation and pain by blocking prostaglandin synthesis.
quick_summary: Long-acting pain reliever for headaches, arthritis, and muscle aches.
who_should_take: Patients needing sustained pain relief for inflammatory conditions.
who_should_not_take: Individuals with a history of stomach ulcers or severe heart disease.
food_interactions: Take with food or milk to reduce stomach upset
alcohol_warning: Increases the risk of stomach bleeding.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme, preventing the formation of angiotensin II
overdose_effects: Severe hypotension, stupor
pregnancy_safety: Category D - Avoid during pregnancy
kidney_liver_warning: Dose adjustment required in kidney impairment
how_it_works_in_body: Relaxes blood vessels, lowering blood pressure and reducing the heart's workload.
quick_summary: Effective blood pressure medication that also protects the heart and kidneys.
who_should_take: Patients with high blood pressure or heart failure.
who_should_not_take: Pregnant women or those with a history of angioedema.
alcohol_warning: May increase the blood pressure-lowering effect, causing dizziness.

mechanism_of_action: Cardioselective beta-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist
overdose_effects: Severe bradycardia, hypotension, heart failure
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Use with caution
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in severe liver impairment
how_it_works_in_body: Slows the heart rate and reduces the force of heart muscle contraction.
quick_summary: Heart medication used to treat high blood pressure, chest pain, and heart failure.
who_should_take: Patients with cardiovascular conditions requiring heart rate control.
who_should_not_take: People with very slow heart rate or severe heart block.
food_interactions: Take with or immediately after meals
alcohol_warning: May increase drowsiness and dizziness.

mechanism_of_action: Increases insulin sensitivity in muscle and adipose tissue
overdose_effects: Hypoglycemia (if taken with other anti-diabetics)
contraindications: Heart failure (NYHA Class III or IV)
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Use with caution
kidney_liver_warning: Avoid in active liver disease
how_it_works_in_body: Helps the body use insulin more effectively to lower blood sugar.
quick_summary: Improves insulin sensitivity to help control blood sugar in Type 2 Diabetes.
who_should_take: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes needing better glycemic control.
who_should_not_take: Individuals with severe heart failure or active bladder cancer.
alcohol_warning: May increase the risk of hypoglycemia.

mechanism_of_action: Stimulates insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells
overdose_effects: Severe hypoglycemia, coma, seizures
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Generally avoided
kidney_liver_warning: Avoid in severe kidney or liver impairment
how_it_works_in_body: Forces the pancreas to release more insulin to lower blood sugar levels.
quick_summary: Oral medication that stimulates the pancreas to produce more insulin.
who_should_take: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes whose pancreas still produces some insulin.
who_should_not_take: Patients with Type 1 Diabetes or severe kidney/liver disease.
food_interactions: Take with meals to prevent low blood sugar
alcohol_warning: Can cause severe hypoglycemia and a disulfiram-like reaction.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 30S ribosomal subunit
overdose_effects: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea
pregnancy_safety: Category D - Avoid during pregnancy
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in liver impairment
how_it_works_in_body: Stops the growth and spread of bacteria.
quick_summary: Broad-spectrum antibiotic used for various bacterial infections and acne.
who_should_take: Patients with susceptible bacterial infections or severe acne.
who_should_not_take: Pregnant women and young children.
food_interactions: Avoid dairy products, antacids, and iron supplements within 2 hours of taking
alcohol_warning: May decrease the effectiveness of the antibiotic.

mechanism_of_action: Synthetic version of the hormone thyroxine (T4) normally produced by thyroid gland.
dosage_common: 25mcg to 200mcg once daily, on empty stomach 30-60 mins before breakfast
overdose_effects: Thyroid storm, rapid heart rate, confusion.
pregnancy_safety: Category A - Safe and essential in pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Safe but regular monitoring required.
how_it_works_in_body: Replaces the missing thyroid hormone to regulate energy and metabolism.
quick_summary: Essential hormone replacement for patients with underactive thyroid.
who_should_take: Patients with hypothyroidism.
who_should_not_take: Patients with untreated adrenal gland problems.
food_interactions: Fiber, walnuts, and soybean flour may decrease absorption.
alcohol_warning: Alcohol does not directly interact but can worsen fatigue.

mechanism_of_action: Relaxes the muscles in the airways and increases air flow to the lungs.
dosage_common: 1-2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed for rescue
overdose_effects: Seizures, chest pain, nausea, extremely fast heartbeat.
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Use if benefit outweighs risk.
kidney_liver_warning: Generally safe.
how_it_works_in_body: Quickly opens up the lung passages during an asthma attack.
quick_summary: Standard rescue inhaler for immediate relief from asthma symptoms.
who_should_take: Patients during acute asthma or COPD flare-ups.
who_should_not_take: Patients with certain heart rhythm disorders without consultation.
food_interactions: None significant.
alcohol_warning: Avoid alcohol as it can trigger asthma symptoms in some people.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2) to reduce pain and inflammation locally.
dosage_common: Apply a thin layer to the affected area 3-4 times daily
overdose_effects: Skin rash, stomach upset (if ingested).
pregnancy_safety: Category C/D - Avoid in late pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Safe topically, but avoid large areas if severe impairment exists.
how_it_works_in_body: Penetrates the skin to reduce inflammation directly at the site of pain.
quick_summary: Widely used topical gel for immediate relief from muscle and joint pain.
who_should_take: People with localized pain, sprains, or arthritic discomfort.
who_should_not_take: People with broken skin or severe aspirin allergy.
food_interactions: Not applicable for topical use.
alcohol_warning: No significant interaction with topical application.

mechanism_of_action: Helps the body absorb calcium and phosphorus, essential for building and maintaining strong bones.
dosage_common: 60,000 IU once weekly for 8-12 weeks, or as directed by a physician.
overdose_effects: Weakness, bone pain, metallic taste, excessive thirst.
pregnancy_safety: Generally safe when used in prescribed amounts.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in kidney disease; monitor calcium levels.
how_it_works_in_body: Converted in the liver and kidneys to its active form, calcitriol, which assists in intestinal calcium absorption.
onset_of_action: Delayed; effects on calcium levels seen over several days.
duration_of_effect: Up to several weeks due to fat-soluble nature.
quick_summary: High-dose Vitamin D supplement used for correction of deficiency and bone health.
who_should_take: Individuals with diagnosed Vitamin D deficiency or bone density issues.
who_should_not_take: Those with hypercalcemia or known hypersensitivity to Vitamin D.
food_interactions: Better absorbed when taken with a fatty meal.
alcohol_warning: No significant interaction, but alcohol can interfere with calcium absorption.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits the fungal enzyme cytochrome P450 14α-demethylase, which is critical for fungal cell membrane synthesis.
dosage_common: 100-200mg once or twice daily depending on infection severity.
overdose_effects: Severe nausea, abdominal pain, visual disturbances.
contraindications: Concurrent use of certain statins or ergot alkaloids
pregnancy_safety: Not recommended during pregnancy unless strictly necessary.
kidney_liver_warning: Caution in liver disease; regular monitoring of liver function tests (LFTs) required.
how_it_works_in_body: Broad-spectrum antifungal that prevents fungal growth by damaging cell membranes.
onset_of_action: Peak concentrations usually reached within 2-5 hours.
duration_of_effect: Long half-life; remains in the body for several days.
quick_summary: Efficient antifungal medication for internal and external fungal infections.
who_should_take: Patients with confirmed fungal infections under medical supervision.
who_should_not_take: Individuals with heart failure or severe liver impairment.
food_interactions: Absorption is increased with acidic food/drinks like cola or juice.
alcohol_warning: Avoid; increases the risk of liver damage.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits the Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme, preventing the production of a substance that narrows blood vessels.
overdose_effects: Severe hypotension, electrolyte disturbances, renal failure.
pregnancy_safety: Unsafe; can cause serious injury or death to the developing fetus.
kidney_liver_warning: Caution in renal impairment; monitoring of potassium and creatinine is essential.
how_it_works_in_body: Relaxes blood vessels and reduces workload on the heart by blocking angiotensin II formation.
quick_summary: Leading medication for blood pressure control and cardiovascular protection.
who_should_take: Adults with hypertension or recovered heart attack patients.
who_should_not_take: Pregnant women or those with a history of serious allergic reactions to ACE inhibitors.
food_interactions: Food does not significantly affect absorption.
alcohol_warning: Can significantly increase the blood-pressure-lowering effect leading to dizziness.

mechanism_of_action: Kills bacteria by preventing them from forming their own protective covering (cell wall).
overdose_effects: Could cause neurological irritation including seizures.
pregnancy_safety: Considered safe, but only use if clearly needed.
kidney_liver_warning: Dosage adjustment might be needed in severe kidney disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Broad-spectrum activity against both Gram-positive and some Gram-negative bacteria.
quick_summary: Potent antibiotic for community-acquired bacterial infections.
who_should_take: Individuals with bacterial infections confirmed by a doctor.
who_should_not_take: Those with history of severe penicillin allergy.
food_interactions: Should be taken after food for better absorption and to reduce stomach upset.
alcohol_warning: No major interaction, but best to avoid during infections.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks L-type and N-type calcium channels, dilating blood vessels and reducing nerve-mediated constriction.
overdose_effects: Severe dizziness, fainting, rapid or slow heartbeat.
pregnancy_safety: Not recommended during pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in patients with liver or kidney dysfunction.
how_it_works_in_body: Unique L/N-type channel blocking prevents reflex tachycardia (racing heart) often seen with other CCBs.
quick_summary: Modern antihypertensive that is gentler on the heart rate compared to older alternatives.
who_should_take: Patients with hypertension, especially those who experience safe swelling with other drugs.
who_should_not_take: Individuals with very low blood pressure or serious heart valve issues.
food_interactions: No major food interactions.
alcohol_warning: Increases risk of low blood pressure and fainting.

mechanism_of_action: Prevents platelets (blood cells) from sticking together and forming dangerous blood clots.
overdose_effects: Excessive bleeding, vomiting blood, black stools.
pregnancy_safety: Generally discouraged unless high risk; consult doctor.
kidney_liver_warning: No major adjustment usually needed, but monitor carefully in liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Irreversibly binds to platelet receptors for the duration of the platelet's life (7-10 days).
onset_of_action: Loading dose takes 2 hours; regular 75mg takes 3-7 days for full effect.
duration_of_effect: Up to 5 days after stopping the drug.
quick_summary: Critical medication for preventing blood clots in heart disease patients.
who_should_take: Individuals with heart stents or history of stroke/heart attack.
who_should_not_take: People with current active bleeding or upcoming major surgery.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
alcohol_warning: Increases the risk of stomach bleeding.

mechanism_of_action: Replaces enzymes that are normally produced by the pancreas to help digest fats, proteins, and sugars.
dosage_common: Varies by meal size; usually 25,000-50,000 units per full meal.
overdose_effects: High uric acid in blood/urine, severe constipation.
contraindications: Known allergy to pork protein (enzymes are porcine-derived)
pregnancy_safety: Generally considered safe as it acts locally in the gut.
kidney_liver_warning: No major warnings; use with medical guidance.
how_it_works_in_body: Enteric-coated microspheres release amylase, protease, and lipase in the small intestine to break down food.
duration_of_effect: Limited to the transit time of food.
quick_summary: Essential replacement therapy for patients who cannot digest food naturally.
who_should_take: Patients with pancreatic insufficiency or chronic digestive issues.
who_should_not_take: Those with pork allergies or acute pancreatic flare-ups.
food_interactions: MUST be taken with every meal and snack to be effective.
alcohol_warning: No direct interaction, but alcohol significantly worsens pancreatic disease.

mechanism_of_action: Reduces cholesterol production in the liver and clears 'bad' cholesterol (LDL) from the blood.
dosage_common: 5mg to 40mg once daily, usually at night.
overdose_effects: Increased muscle pain symptoms, nausea.
pregnancy_safety: UNSAFE; causes significant risk of birth defects.
kidney_liver_warning: Monitor LFTs regularly; check kidney function at high doses.
how_it_works_in_body: Potent statin that significantly lowers LDL and raises 'good' HDL cholesterol.
duration_of_effect: Longer-acting statin; blood levels remain significant for 24 hours.
quick_summary: Gold-standard medication for lowering cholesterol and preventing heart disease.
who_should_take: Individuals with high LDL levels or cardiovascular risk factors.
who_should_not_take: Pregnant women or those with active liver inflammation.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
alcohol_warning: Limit; increases risk of liver issues and muscle pain.

mechanism_of_action: Rabeprazole reduces stomach acid; Domperidone increases gut movement to prevent reflux.
dosage_common: One capsule daily, 30 minutes before breakfast.
overdose_effects: Agitation, confusion, drowsiness, seizures (rare).
pregnancy_safety: Use only if essential; consult doctor.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in moderate to severe liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Provides 24-hour acid suppression and improves gastric emptying.
onset_of_action: Acid relief in 1 hour; motion effect in 30-60 mins.
quick_summary: Effective combination for treating severe acidity accompanied by nausea.
who_should_take: Patients with acid reflux who also experience bloating or nausea.
who_should_not_take: Patients with existing heart rhythm conditions.
food_interactions: MUST be taken on an empty stomach for maximum effect.
alcohol_warning: Avoid; increases acid secretion and reduces drug effectiveness.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits the synthesis of ergosterol, a vital component of fungal cell membranes, leading to fungal cell death.
dosage_common: 100-200mg once or twice daily, depending on the severity.
overdose_effects: Severe nausea, liver issues, allergic reactions.
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Avoid unless strictly necessary.
kidney_liver_warning: Requires close monitoring in liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Stops the fungus from growing and replicating by destroying its cell wall.
quick_summary: A potent antifungal used for stubborn skin and nail fungal infections.
who_should_take: Patients with fungal infections as prescribed by a dermatologist or physician.
who_should_not_take: Patients with a history of congestive heart failure.
food_interactions: Better absorbed when taken with a full meal or acidic drinks like cola (if patient has low stomach acid).
alcohol_warning: Avoid excessive alcohol due to potential liver strain.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks both beta-1, beta-2, and alpha-1 receptors, lowering heart rate and dilating blood vessels to reduce blood pressure.
dosage_common: Starts at 3.125mg or 6.25mg twice daily, adjusted by a doctor.
overdose_effects: Extreme low blood pressure, fainting, difficulty breathing.
pregnancy_safety: Category C/D - Consult your doctor; beta blockers can affect fetal blood flow.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in liver impairment.
how_it_works_in_body: Reduces the workload of the heart and helps it pump more efficiently.
quick_summary: A specialized blood pressure medication that also helps in heart failure management.
who_should_take: Patients with high blood pressure or heart failure as prescribed.
who_should_not_take: Patients with severe asthma or very low heart rate.
food_interactions: Should be taken with food to reduce the risk of a sudden drop in blood pressure.
alcohol_warning: Avoid; can cause sudden, dangerous drops in blood pressure.

mechanism_of_action: Transports long-chain fatty acids into mitochondria, where they are oxidized for energy production.
overdose_effects: Diarrhea and stomach pain.
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Generally considered safe; consulted if necessary.
kidney_liver_warning: Safe in kidney disease; often used in dialysis patients.
how_it_works_in_body: Helps the body convert fat into energy more efficiently.
quick_summary: A supplement that helps the body use energy and supports heart and muscle health.
who_should_take: People with carnitine deficiency or specific metabolic needs.
who_should_not_take: People with a history of seizures without medical supervision.
food_interactions: Best taken with or after food.
alcohol_warning: No major interaction, but moderate use is advised.

mechanism_of_action: Contains Sacubitril (neprilysin inhibitor) and Valsartan (ARB). It increases protective hormones and blocks harmful ones to improve heart function.
dosage_common: Starts low (e.g. 50mg or 100mg) and increases to 200mg twice daily.
overdose_effects: Extreme low blood pressure, severe dehydration.
drug_interactions: ACE inhibitors (must wait 36 hours after stopping)
pregnancy_safety: Category D - STRICTLY AVOID; can cause fetal death.
kidney_liver_warning: Requires close monitoring of kidney function.
how_it_works_in_body: Dual action reduces strain on the heart and prevents fluid buildup.
quick_summary: A breakthrough medication for managing advanced heart failure and improving survival.
who_should_take: Patients with specific types of chronic heart failure prescribed by cardiologists.
who_should_not_take: Pregnant women or those with a history of severe swelling reactions.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
alcohol_warning: Avoid; increases risk of dangerous low blood pressure.

mechanism_of_action: Inhibits blood clotting factors (mainly Factor Xa and IIa) to prevent the formation of blood clots.
dosage_common: Often 40mg once daily for prevention, higher for treatment (subcutaneous injection).
overdose_effects: Severe, uncontrollable bleeding.
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Generally considered safe and often used when anticoagulation is needed in pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Requires dose adjustment in severe kidney disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Keeps blood thin and flowing, preventing dangerous clots from clogging up arteries or veins.
quick_summary: An essential injectable blood thinner used to prevent and treat dangerous blood clots.
who_should_take: People at high risk of blood clots, e.g., post-surgery or during hospitalization.
who_should_not_take: Individuals with active bleeding or severe bleeding disorders.
food_interactions: No major food interactions; strictly injectable.
alcohol_warning: Avoid; increases the risk of internal bleeding.

mechanism_of_action: Selectively blocks beta-1 receptors in the heart, reducing heart rate and blood pressure without significantly affecting the lungs.
overdose_effects: Extreme fatigue, very slow heart rate, fainting.
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Consult doctor; may reduce placental blood flow.
kidney_liver_warning: Safe in most but check with doctor if severe.
how_it_works_in_body: Makes the heart beat slower and with less force, lowering blood pressure and heart workload.
quick_summary: A highly selective beta blocker used for blood pressure and heart protection.
who_should_take: Patients with hypertension or heart disease as prescribed.
who_should_not_take: People with very slow heart rate or severe heart conditions.
food_interactions: Can be taken with or without food.
alcohol_warning: Limit; can increase dizziness and drowsiness.

mechanism_of_action: Contains Lipase, Protease, and Amylase which replace natural pancreatic enzymes to help digest fats, proteins, and sugars.
dosage_common: Taken with every meal and snack; dose depends on meals.
overdose_effects: Severe stomach pain and digestive distress.
pregnancy_safety: Category C - Consult doctor; generally considered low risk if clinically indicated.
kidney_liver_warning: Safe for most patients.
how_it_works_in_body: Provides the enzymes necessary for food breakdown when the pancreas isn't working correctly.
quick_summary: Essential enzyme replacement for people who cannot digest food due to pancreas problems.
who_should_take: Patients with chronic pancreatitis or digestive enzyme deficiency.
who_should_not_take: People allergic to pork products (enzymes are porcine-derived).
food_interactions: MUST be taken with food. Do not skip when eating.
alcohol_warning: Alcohol severely worsens underlying pancreatitis; strictly avoid if you have it.

mechanism_of_action: Blocks the hydrogen-potassium ATPase pump in the stomach lining, effectively stopping acid production.
dosage_common: 20mg once daily, preferably 30 min before breakfast.
overdose_effects: Nausea, vomiting, confusion, increased heart rate.
pregnancy_safety: Category B - Generally safe; consult doctor.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in severe liver disease.
how_it_works_in_body: Directly stops the source of acid production, providing relief from burning and pain.
quick_summary: A powerful antacid used for severe acidity, ulcers, and acid reflux.
who_should_take: People with chronic acidity or stomach ulcers as prescribed.
who_should_not_take: Patients with a known allergy to rabeprazole or similar medicines.
food_interactions: Most effective on an empty stomach, 30 minutes before breakfast.
alcohol_warning: Avoid; alcohol increases stomach acid and worsens symptoms.

mechanism_of_action: Provides essential calcium for bone strength and Vitamin D3 (Cholecalciferol) to help the body absorb the calcium.
dosage_common: 1-2 tablets daily or as directed by a doctor.
overdose_effects: Nausea, vomiting, thirst, constipation, and muscle weakness.
pregnancy_safety: Category A - Safe and often recommended during pregnancy.
kidney_liver_warning: Use with caution in kidney disease due to risk of stones.
how_it_works_in_body: Replenishes the body's calcium stores and maintains bone density.
quick_summary: A common calcium and vitamin D supplement for bone health.
who_should_take: People with poor calcium intake or bone thinning.
who_should_not_take: People with high calcium levels in blood or recurring kidney stones.
food_interactions: Best taken after meals for better absorption and less stomach upset.
alcohol_warning: No major interaction, but high alcohol can decrease bone density.
